Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic. I'd have bursts where I'd listen to lecture recordings while doing other things, but I never really sat down with a proper schedule. I have access to all these resources at home, but I'd often just let them pile up and then cram when I felt like it. The mindfulness exercises I started doing helped me focus a bit, but organizing my actual study time? That remained a mess throughout the course.
Q2: My pattern varied wildly depending on what else was going on in my life. I'd go weeks barely touching anything, then suddenly realize deadlines were approaching and scramble. With my extracurriculars taking up time and my anxiety spiking randomly, regular study times just never happened for me. I know I should have been more consistent, but I just couldn't make myself stick to a schedule.
Q3: Usually it was panic, if I'm being honest. I'd suddenly remember an assignment was due or get a notification about something and rush to log in. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if I'd missed anything important since I wasn't attending classes much. The anxiety of not knowing what was happening would build up until I finally forced myself to look.
Q4: Not really, no. The LMS never became a natural part of my day. I'd have to consciously remind myself to check it, and even then I'd often put it off. The closest it got to routine was maybe logging in on Sunday nights to see what the week ahead looked like, but even that was inconsistent. I just couldn't make it feel automatic.
Q5: The audio recordings and video lectures were my lifeline since I learn best by listening. I could put them on while doing other things or lying in bed when my anxiety was too high to sit at a desk. The discussion boards were occasionally helpful too because I could see what confused other people and realize I wasn't alone. Those resources felt like they actually taught me something rather than just checking boxes.
Q6: I basically ignored the reading materials and PDFs most of the time. They just sat there unopened because reading dense text makes my anxiety worse and I can't absorb information that way. The optional practice quizzes I also avoided because they felt like extra pressure I didn't need. If something wasn't going to be graded, it usually got pushed aside.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could immediately see how it connected to an assignment or exam. If I could listen to it rather than read it, even better. I also valued things that didn't take forever—short, focused content that got to the point. When something felt like busy work with no clear purpose, I'd mentally check out even if I technically completed it.
Q8: Optional activities felt like suggestions I could safely ignore, which I usually did. Required ones at least had the pressure of grades behind them, so I'd drag myself through those. But honestly, even required activities sometimes felt optional in my head until the deadline was breathing down my neck. The distinction mattered less than how much mental energy I had on any given day.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded a ton of audio files and listened to them on my phone while walking or lying down. I also used YouTube videos from other educators when the course material wasn't clicking for me. My notes were all in a physical notebook that I'd review away from my computer. A lot of my actual learning happened completely offline.
Q10: There were many nights where I'd be reviewing my handwritten notes or re-listening to downloaded lectures on my phone with the LMS nowhere in sight. Sometimes I'd discuss concepts with friends from my extracurricular activities who were in similar courses. The LMS would show me as inactive, but I was actually processing and studying the material in my own way.
Q11: Yes, constantly. I'd download a lecture recording once, then listen to it three or four more times on my phone over the next week. The LMS only registered that initial download, not all the times I replayed it while trying to fall asleep or during my commute. Same with PDFs I'd save and occasionally actually read on my tablet offline.
Q12: Mid-semester was brutal. My anxiety was through the roof, I was barely attending classes, and everything felt overwhelming. The mindfulness intervention helped lift my mood eventually, but there were weeks where just opening the LMS felt like too much. When my stress peaked, I'd completely disengage and then feel worse about falling behind, which made engaging even harder.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted from extracurriculars or paralyzed by anxiety, the LMS became this thing I actively avoided. Competing responsibilities meant I'd prioritize whatever was screaming loudest, and the LMS doesn't really scream—it just sits there. Confusion about what I'd missed from not attending made logging in feel like facing a mountain of catch-up I couldn't handle.
Q14: So many times. I'd scroll through materials with my eyes glazed over, technically looking at them but absorbing nothing. Sometimes I'd have a video playing while my mind was completely elsewhere, worrying about other things. The anxiety made it hard to actually be present even when I was physically engaging with the content. It was like going through the motions without being there.
Q15: I postponed discussion posts constantly because they required me to think coherently and put myself out there, which my anxiety made terrifying. I'd also avoid starting assignments until the last possible moment because beginning felt harder than continuing. I knew these things were important, but the mental barrier to starting was just so high that I'd find any excuse to delay.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to act. I'd ignore everything until the deadline was imminent, then frantically complete whatever was due. My LMS activity probably looked like nothing, nothing, nothing, then a sudden spike of panic-driven engagement. It's not healthy, but that pressure was the only thing that could override my avoidance.
Q17: Feedback had a complicated effect on me. Good feedback would give me a temporary boost, but then I'd feel pressure to maintain that standard. Critical feedback would send my anxiety spiraling and make me avoid that type of work even more. I'd sometimes delay checking my grades for days because I was scared of what I'd see. The feedback itself was useful, but emotionally it was a lot to handle.
Q18: The structure felt like it assumed I was attending regularly and keeping up week by week, which I wasn't. When I'd fall behind, the structure made it hard to know where to jump back in. The flexibility to complete assignments on my own timeline helped, but the sequential nature of the content meant missing early stuff made later stuff confusing. I needed more scaffolding for people like me who drift in and out.
Q19: Having audio and video options made engagement easier for my learning style. But the sheer volume of content made it harder—there was always so much to catch up on. The discussion requirements felt forced and added pressure without adding much value for me personally. More flexibility in how we could demonstrate understanding would have helped me engage on my own terms.
Q20: I think those students might be like me in some ways—logging in, clicking around, trying to figure out what's going on, but being too overwhelmed or confused to actually produce work. Or they might be looking for specific information they need without engaging with everything else. High activity doesn't mean productive activity; sometimes it's just anxious scrolling.
Q21: That's basically me. I barely logged in, my attendance was terrible, but I still got decent grades because I was learning in ways the system couldn't see. Downloaded materials, external resources, studying with friends, listening to recordings repeatedly offline—none of that shows up. The LMS saw a disengaged student, but I was actually engaging with the content, just not through their platform.
Q22: Course logs can show when you logged in and what you clicked on, which is useful for tracking basic activity patterns. But they completely miss the quality of that engagement and all the learning that happens offline. They can't capture whether someone was actually thinking while watching a video or just letting it play. The emotional and cognitive aspects of learning are invisible to the data.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks to get them done. It's when you're curious and thinking critically, even if that thinking happens while you're lying in bed staring at the ceiling. For me, engagement is more about mental investment than physical presence or clicking through an LMS. Sometimes my most engaged moments happened nowhere near a computer.
Q24: The LMS can't show that I was genuinely trying despite my terrible attendance and low login frequency. It can't capture my anxiety making simple tasks feel impossible, or the mindfulness work I did to get through the course. It doesn't know about the hours I spent with downloaded lectures or discussing concepts with friends. My grades show I learned something, but the journey to get there was way more complicated than any data could reveal.
